narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 40% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This prediction draws from key details in your profile, like your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, which is below the typical 3.21 average; your LSAT score of 36, slightly under the usual 36.7; and your position in the bottom decile for both first-year and third-year school grades, far from the middle-of-the-pack averages around 5.7. As a Black male from a middle-income family planning full-time studies, you're in a group that's less common in the dataset—Blacks make up just 4.6% compared to 83.8% Whites, and males are 56.4%—but these demographics played a role too. The model's decision was most heavily swayed by your third-year grade decile, which strongly nudged the prediction toward expecting a bar failure rather than a pass. Right behind that was your LSAT score, followed closely by your first-year grade decile, each pushing in the same concerning direction. Your family income bracket, racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender rounded out the influences in that order, all tilting the odds toward failure to a lesser but still notable degree. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have stronger grades and GPAs placing them mid-pack or higher, your academic markers stood out as the biggest gaps. None of these factors pulled strongly in your favor, which is why the overall risk seemed too high for admission. Looking ahead, you can realistically boost your bar passing odds by targeting what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim above the average, strengthen your academic habits to climb those grade deciles in future coursework, and seek targeted prep resources proven for bar success. Building a stronger GPA through additional undergrad classes or a post-bac program could also help if applicable. With focused effort on these academics, students like you have turned similar profiles around—keep pushing, and reapply when you've shored up those strengths. We're rooting for your success.
